scenarioStory.history: 72-year-old male with severe COPD (GOLD stage IV per pulmonologist notes on fridge), on continuous home oxygen 2 L/min nasal cannula for the past 3 years. Reports a head cold/upper respiratory infection for the past 4 days with productive cough of yellow-green sputum. Dyspnea has been worsening over the last 48 hours despite using his albuterol inhaler 'every couple hours.' Today he can no longer walk to the bathroom without stopping to rest. Wife called 911 when he seemed 'sleepier than usual' and was leaning forward over the kitchen table to breathe. Patient is a 50-pack-year smoker, quit 8 years ago. Two prior hospital admissions for COPD exacerbation in the past 12 months — neither required intubation. Baseline SpO2 on his home pulse oximeter runs 88-92% on his 2 L. Recently finished a 5-day prednisone taper from his PCP one week ago. Denies chest pain, denies leg swelling, denies orthopnea (sleeps with one pillow at baseline).
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: Crew arrives to find a 72-year-old male in obvious moderate-to-severe respiratory distress, seated in tripod position at the kitchen table. He is on his home O2 at 2 L/min via nasal cannula. He speaks in 2-3 word phrases with audible wheezing and prolonged expiratory phase. He is alert but mildly drowsy (GCS 14 — eye-opening to voice rather than spontaneous, otherwise normal). Skin is warm, dusky-pink, mildly diaphoretic. Monitor reveals sinus tachycardia at 108, BP 142/86, RR 28, SpO2 91% on his home 2 L NC, and an EtCO2 of 62 mmHg with a clear shark-fin waveform — this is the diagnostic finding. He is hypercarbic and ventilating poorly, but his SpO2 of 91% IS his baseline. The temptation is to crank up the oxygen because '91% looks low' — but his real problem is CO2 retention/ventilatory failure, not hypoxia. He needs ventilatory support (NIV/CPAP) with bronchodilators in-line, not more O2. → CPAP + DuoNeb working: Approximately 5-7 minutes after initiating CPAP with albuterol/ipratropium nebulized in-line, the patient is responding. EtCO2 has trended down from 62 → 58 → 50 mmHg as ventilation improves. Respiratory rate has slowed from 28 → 22. SpO2 sits at a comfortable 93% (within his acceptable range; crew titrated FiO2 modestly rather than aggressively). GCS has improved from 14 to 15 — he is more conversational and brighter. However, the CPAP mask is uncomfortable. He is anxious on the mask, repeatedly tries to pull it off, and is borderline non-compliant. The crew must coach him, maintain the mask seal, and recognize that anxiety on NIV is almost always inadequate ventilatory support or insufficient explanation — NOT a sedation indication per protocol.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Worsening shortness of breath, can't catch my breath

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: Crew arrives to find a 72-year-old male in obvious moderate-to-severe respiratory distress, seated in tripod position at the kitchen table. He is on his home O2 at 2 L/min via nasal cannula. He speaks in 2-3 word phrases with audible wheezing and prolonged expiratory phase. He is alert but mildly drowsy (GCS 14 — eye-opening to voice rather than spontaneous, otherwise normal). Skin is warm, dusky-pink, mildly diaphoretic. Monitor reveals sinus tachycardia at 108, BP 142/86, RR 28, SpO2 91% on his home 2 L NC, and an EtCO2 of 62 mmHg with a clear shark-fin waveform — this is the diagnostic finding. He is hypercarbic and ventilating poorly, but his SpO2 of 91% IS his baseline. The temptation is to crank up the oxygen because '91% looks low' — but his real problem is CO2 retention/ventilatory failure, not hypoxia. He needs ventilatory support (NIV/CPAP) with bronchodilators in-line, not more O2.
relatedChecklist: Primary survey; recognize severe respiratory distress with impending failure
Targeted SAMPLE; identify COPD, home O2, baseline SpO2 88-92%, recent URI
Place waveform EtCO2; recognize EtCO2 62 + shark-fin = bronchospasm + hypoventilation
Target SpO2 88-92% (his baseline) — DO NOT over-oxygenate this CO2 retainer
Initiate CPAP for severe respiratory distress / impending failure; coach patient
Albuterol 5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized IN-LINE with NIV
Establish IV access for steroid administration and ongoing care
Dexamethasone 16 mg IV (78 kg × 0.6 mg/kg = 46.8 mg → cap at 16 mg max)
Continuous cardiac monitor and 12-lead EKG

scenarioEvents.name: CPAP + DuoNeb working
scenarioEvents.description: Approximately 5-7 minutes after initiating CPAP with albuterol/ipratropium nebulized in-line, the patient is responding. EtCO2 has trended down from 62 → 58 → 50 mmHg as ventilation improves. Respiratory rate has slowed from 28 → 22. SpO2 sits at a comfortable 93% (within his acceptable range; crew titrated FiO2 modestly rather than aggressively). GCS has improved from 14 to 15 — he is more conversational and brighter. However, the CPAP mask is uncomfortable. He is anxious on the mask, repeatedly tries to pull it off, and is borderline non-compliant. The crew must coach him, maintain the mask seal, and recognize that anxiety on NIV is almost always inadequate ventilatory support or insufficient explanation — NOT a sedation indication per protocol.
relatedChecklist: Maintain mask seal; do NOT break CPAP circuit during transport
Coach patient through NIV anxiety; treat with ventilatory support, not sedation
Document EtCO2 trend (62→58→50) and SpO2 staying in 88-93% target
Continue albuterol 5 mg nebulized in-line; unlimited frequency while in distress
Transport on CPAP; advance notification to receiving ED

scenarioEvents.name: Over-oxygenated, narcotizing
scenarioEvents.description: Crew misread the 91% SpO2 as primary hypoxia and slapped a non-rebreather at 15 L/min onto the patient. SpO2 quickly climbs to 99% — looks great on the screen. But the patient's hypoxic respiratory drive (which had been compensating for chronically high CO2) is now blunted. Over the next 4-6 minutes ventilation deteriorates: RR drops from 28 to 16 (looks reassuring but is actually hypoventilation), EtCO2 climbs from 62 → 72 → 80. The patient becomes progressively drowsier — GCS slides from 14 to 11 to 9. This is classic hypercapnic narcosis (CO2 narcosis), and the crew was misled by an SpO2 number that 'looked normal.' Recovery requires recognizing the trap: drop the FiO2 back toward baseline, initiate NIV urgently (ventilatory support, not more O2), and prepare for BVM/intubation if NIV fails.
relatedChecklist: Recognize hypercapnic narcosis: SpO2 looks great but EtCO2 80 + GCS dropping
Wean O2 back toward baseline target (88-92%); remove NRB
Initiate CPAP/BiPAP urgently; bronchodilator in-line
If GCS continues to fall / NIV intolerant: BVM with PEEP at adult rate 10-12/min

scenarioEvents.name: CPAP without DuoNeb
scenarioEvents.description: Crew correctly recognized the need for NIV and started CPAP — but did NOT put albuterol/ipratropium in-line. Positive pressure is now fighting an obstructed airway. The patient experiences worsening discomfort on the mask (the bronchospasm makes exhalation against PEEP harder), repeatedly tries to pull the mask off, and the EtCO2 trend is poor: 62 → 60 → 60 (essentially flat, not improving). The shark-fin waveform persists. Without bronchodilator the underlying obstructive physiology is unchanged, and CPAP alone is insufficient to fix it. Recovery: place a nebulizer in-line via T-connector with spring valve, coach the patient, maintain seal.
relatedChecklist: Dexamethasone 16 mg IV (78 kg × 0.6 mg/kg = 46.8 mg → cap at 16 mg max)
Recognize NIV alone insufficient — bronchospasm untreated, shark fin persists
Add albuterol 5 mg + ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized IN-LINE via T-connector
Coach patient, do NOT break the mask seal to attempt a separate neb
